exaggeration.definition: A statement that represents something as better, worse, or more extreme than it really is.
examples: Calling the tiny hill a mountain was an exaggeration.
She has a habit of exaggeration when telling stories about her trip.

marine.definition: Relating to the sea or ocean.
examples: The aquarium specializes in marine life from the Pacific.
He studied marine biology at a coastal university.

organisms.definition: Individual living things, such as animals, plants, or microbes.
examples: The pond was full of tiny organisms visible only under a microscope.
Scientists classify organisms based on their shared characteristics.

camouflaged.definition: Disguised to blend in with the surroundings.
examples: The moth was so well camouflaged against the bark that it seemed invisible.
Soldiers wore camouflaged uniforms to avoid detection.

crevices.definition: Narrow openings or cracks, especially in rock.
examples: Small crabs hid in the crevices along the tide pool.
Water seeped slowly into the crevices of the old stone wall.

polyps.definition: Small, soft-bodied aquatic animals, often living in colonies, that can form coral.
examples: Each coral polyp is smaller than a fingernail.
The polyps retracted quickly when the diver's shadow passed over them.
The gland secretes a hormone that regulates growth.
Certain trees secrete a sticky sap when their bark is cut.

sprawling.definition: Spreading out over a large area in an irregular way.
examples: The sprawling city stretched far beyond its original borders.
A sprawling oak tree shaded most of the backyard.

algae.definition: Simple, often aquatic organisms that can photosynthesize, ranging from single cells to large seaweeds.
examples: Green algae covered the surface of the still pond.
Some algae grow so quickly they can cover an entire lake.

photosynthesis.definition: The process by which plants and some organisms use sunlight to convert carbon dioxide and water into energy.
examples: Photosynthesis allows leaves to turn sunlight into usable energy.
Without enough light, the plant's rate of photosynthesis slows dramatically.

vulnerability.definition: The quality of being easily harmed or affected.
examples: The old bridge's vulnerability to flooding worried engineers.
Sharing her fears with a friend felt like an act of vulnerability.

bleaching.definition: The process of losing color, especially coral losing its algae due to stress.
examples: Rising ocean temperatures have accelerated coral bleaching worldwide.
Sunlight can cause bleaching of fabric left outside too long.

devastate.definition: To severely damage or destroy something.
examples: The storm threatened to devastate the coastal town.
A single bad season can devastate a small farm's income.

ecosystems.definition: Communities of living organisms interacting with their environment.
examples: Wetlands are ecosystems that support an unusual mix of species.
Removing one predator can throw an entire ecosystem out of balance.

resilient.definition: Able to recover quickly from difficulties.
examples: Despite the drought, the old vineyard proved remarkably resilient.
Children can be surprisingly resilient after a difficult move.

tolerate.definition: To endure or withstand something without being harmed.
examples: This variety of wheat can tolerate long periods of drought.
He could barely tolerate the noise from the construction site.

restore.definition: To bring something back to its original condition.
examples: The team worked for years to restore the damaged wetland.
She hoped a good night's sleep would restore her energy.

commercial.definition: Relating to buying and selling, or produced for profit rather than at home.
examples: Commercial yeast made bread baking faster and more consistent.
The bakery switched from a home recipe to a commercial one.

microorganisms.definition: Tiny living things, such as bacteria or yeast, visible only under a microscope.
examples: Billions of microorganisms live in a single gram of healthy soil.
Fermented foods rely on beneficial microorganisms.

colonize.definition: To establish a population in a new area.
examples: Wild yeast will slowly colonize a bowl of flour and water.
Moss began to colonize the damp, shaded side of the roof.

thriving.definition: Growing or developing vigorously and successfully.
examples: The starter needs daily feeding to keep its yeast thriving.
Their small bakery grew into a thriving neighborhood business.

gluten.definition: A protein found in wheat that gives dough its elastic texture.
examples: Kneading dough helps develop the gluten that traps gas bubbles.
Some people avoid gluten due to a sensitivity or allergy.

distinctive.definition: Having a quality that makes something easily recognizable as different.
examples: Sourdough has a distinctive tang that regular bread lacks.
Her handwriting has a distinctive slant that's easy to spot.

identical.definition: Exactly the same in every detail.
examples: No two sourdough starters produce identical loaves of bread.
The twins wore identical outfits to the party.

humid.definition: Having a high level of moisture in the air.
examples: A humid kitchen can make bread dough rise faster.
Summers in the region are hot and humid.

nurtured.definition: Cared for and encouraged the growth or development of something.
examples: She nurtured the starter for weeks before it was ready to bake with.
His early interest in music was nurtured by a patient teacher.

heirlooms.definition: Valuable objects passed down through generations.
examples: The recipe box had become a treasured family heirloom.
Bakers sometimes pass small portions of their starters down like heirlooms.

fermentation.definition: A chemical process in which microorganisms break down sugars, often producing gas, acid, or alcohol.
examples: Long fermentation gives sourdough its characteristic flavor.
Fermentation is also used to make yogurt and cheese.

advocates.definition: People who publicly support or recommend a particular cause or idea.
examples: Advocates of slow fermentation argue it makes bread easier to digest.
Local advocates pushed the city to build a new park.

predictable.definition: Behaving in a way that is expected or easy to foresee.
examples: Commercial yeast makes baking times far more predictable.
His reaction to bad news was entirely predictable.

tang.definition: A strong, sharp flavor or smell.
examples: The bread had a pleasant sourdough tang.
A squeeze of lime gave the dish a fresh tang.

culture.definition: A growth of microorganisms maintained for a particular purpose.
examples: The bakery kept a sourdough culture alive for over a decade.
Scientists grew a bacterial culture in the lab dish.

residents.definition: People who live in a particular place.
examples: Local residents gathered outside to watch the unusual sky.
The new residents introduced themselves to their neighbors.

rippled.definition: Moved in small waves or wavelike patterns.
examples: Curtains of green light rippled slowly across the night sky.
The wind rippled across the surface of the lake.

confined.definition: Restricted to a particular area or limit.
examples: The aurora is usually confined to regions near the poles.
He was confined to bed rest for a week after the surgery.

funnels.definition: Directs something through a narrow channel toward a particular point.
examples: Earth's magnetic field funnels charged particles toward the poles.
The narrow canyon funnels wind straight through the valley.

collide.definition: To crash or come together with force.
examples: Charged particles collide with oxygen molecules high in the atmosphere.
The two cyclists nearly collided at the intersection.

molecules.definition: The smallest units of a substance that retain its chemical properties, made of two or more atoms.
examples: Oxygen and nitrogen molecules make up most of Earth's atmosphere.
Water molecules expand slightly as they freeze.
The sun occasionally unleashes a burst of charged particles into space.
The storm unleashed heavy rain across the entire coast.

eruption.definition: A sudden, often violent release or outburst.
examples: A coronal mass ejection is an eruption of solar material into space.
The volcano's eruption sent ash miles into the sky.

geomagnetic.definition: Relating to the magnetism of the Earth.
examples: A strong geomagnetic storm can push the aurora far from the poles.
Scientists track geomagnetic activity to protect satellites.
The solar flare triggered a rare aurora display over Ohio.
A loud noise triggered the dog's barking.

sunspots.definition: Temporary dark, cooler patches that appear on the surface of the sun.
examples: A cluster of sunspots released a rapid sequence of flares.
Astronomers have tracked sunspot cycles for centuries.

latitudes.definition: Distances north or south of the equator, measured in degrees.
examples: The aurora became visible at latitudes that rarely see it.
Plants adapted to cold climates thrive at higher latitudes.

spectacle.definition: A visually striking or dramatic public display.
examples: For most observers, the glowing sky was a harmless spectacle.
The fireworks display was a spectacle the whole town remembered.

disrupt.definition: To interrupt or disturb the normal function of something.
examples: A strong geomagnetic storm can disrupt satellites and radio signals.
Construction noise disrupted the meeting all afternoon.

infrastructure.definition: The basic physical systems a society depends on, such as power grids and communication networks.
examples: Utility companies monitor space weather to protect critical infrastructure.
The city invested heavily in repairing its aging infrastructure.

mottled.definition: Marked with spots or patches of different colors.
examples: The octopus's mottled brown skin matched the rocks perfectly.
Sunlight fell in mottled patches through the leaves.

specialized.definition: Designed or trained for a particular purpose.
examples: Chromatophores are specialized skin cells that control pigment.
The hospital has a specialized unit for treating burns.

pigment.definition: A substance that gives color to skin, tissue, or other material.
examples: Chromatophores expand to reveal more pigment beneath the skin.
Artists once made pigment from crushed minerals.

muscular.definition: Relating to or produced by the muscles.
examples: Chromatophores are controlled by direct muscular action, not hormones.
Years of swimming gave her a strong, muscular build.

shimmering.definition: Shining with a soft, flickering light.
examples: Iridophores produce a shimmering blue-green sheen on the octopus's skin.
The lake had a shimmering surface in the afternoon sun.

scatter.definition: To spread or distribute something widely or unevenly.
examples: Leucophores scatter light to help create the color white.
The wind began to scatter the fallen leaves across the yard.

camouflage.definition: Coloring or a pattern that helps something blend into its surroundings.
examples: An octopus uses camouflage to disappear against the coral.
The soldiers used natural camouflage to stay hidden in the brush.

predators.definition: Animals that hunt and eat other animals.
examples: Camouflage helps octopuses hide from predators.
Zebras rely on speed to escape predators on the open plain.

aggression.definition: Hostile or forceful behavior, often as a warning or threat.
examples: Some color changes appear to signal aggression toward a rival.
The dog's raised fur was a sign of aggression.

courtship.definition: Behavior intended to attract a mate.
examples: Researchers observed color changes during what looked like courtship.
The bird's elaborate dance is part of its courtship display.

colorblind.definition: Unable to perceive certain colors normally.
examples: Octopuses are considered colorblind by conventional eye tests.
He is colorblind and has trouble distinguishing red from green.

conventional.definition: Based on what is generally done or believed; standard.
examples: By conventional tests, octopus eyes cannot detect color.
She preferred an unconventional approach over the conventional method.

embedded.definition: Fixed firmly and deeply within something.
examples: Light-sensitive proteins may be embedded directly in the octopus's skin.
A small tracking chip was embedded under the animal's skin.

perceive.definition: To become aware of or notice something through the senses.
examples: The octopus may match colors it cannot consciously perceive.
It took her a moment to perceive the change in his tone.

investigated.definition: Examined or studied carefully in order to understand something.
examples: Scientists have investigated how octopus skin might sense light.
Police investigated the cause of the warehouse fire.

audacious.definition: Showing a bold, daring willingness to take risks.
examples: Promising ten-day cross-country mail delivery by horseback was an audacious plan.
The audacious young pilot volunteered for the most dangerous route.

relays.definition: Groups or stages of people or animals that take turns completing part of a longer task.
examples: Riders passed the mail pouch along in a chain of relays.
The charity race was run in relays, with each runner covering one mile.

mochila.definition: A lightweight leather saddlebag used to carry mail across a horse's saddle.
examples: The rider transferred the mochila to a fresh horse in seconds.
A mochila had four pockets, one of which was always locked.

exhaustion.definition: A state of extreme physical or mental tiredness.
examples: Days of continuous riding pushed the messengers to the point of exhaustion.
She collapsed on the couch from sheer exhaustion after the move.

obsolete.definition: No longer used or needed because something newer has replaced it.
examples: The telegraph made the horseback mail relay obsolete almost overnight.
That model of phone became obsolete within a few years.

transcontinental.definition: Extending or traveling across an entire continent.
examples: The transcontinental telegraph connected the east and west coasts by wire.
They took a transcontinental flight from New York to Tokyo.

magnetron.definition: A vacuum tube that generates microwaves, used in radar and microwave ovens.
examples: The magnetron inside a microwave oven produces the waves that heat food.
Early radar systems depended on a powerful magnetron to generate signals.

agitating.definition: Causing something to move about vigorously, often to mix or disturb it.
examples: Microwaves cook food partly by agitating the water molecules inside it.
The washing machine cleans clothes by agitating them in soapy water.

miniaturization.definition: The process of making something much smaller while keeping its function.
examples: Decades of miniaturization turned the room-sized computer into a handheld device.
Miniaturization eventually made microwave ovens small enough for home kitchens.

splattering.definition: Scattering small drops or particles of a liquid or soft substance messily.
examples: The exploding egg ended up splattering across the workbench.
Paint went splattering across the floor when the can tipped over.

hunch.definition: An intuitive feeling about something without firm evidence.
examples: Acting on a hunch, he aimed the device at a bag of popcorn.
Her hunch about the missing files turned out to be correct.

corroded.definition: Gradually worn away or damaged by chemical reaction, often with metal.
examples: Centuries underwater left the bronze mechanism badly corroded.
The old pipes had corroded until they finally began to leak.

fragments.definition: Small parts broken off from something larger.
examples: Researchers pieced together dozens of bronze fragments to study the device.
Fragments of the ancient pot were scattered across the dig site.

intricate.definition: Very complicated or detailed in design.
examples: The device contained an intricate system of interlocking gears.
She admired the intricate pattern carved into the old door.

sophistication.definition: A high level of complexity, refinement, or advanced development.
examples: The gearing revealed a level of sophistication no one expected from that era.
The design showed real sophistication despite its simple materials.

craftsmen.definition: Skilled workers who make things by hand, especially with traditional techniques.
examples: Ancient craftsmen must have spent months building the intricate mechanism.
Local craftsmen still build furniture using centuries-old methods.

elaborate.definition: Involving many carefully arranged parts or steps; detailed and complex.
examples: Slicing an onion sets off an elaborate chemical chain reaction.
They planned an elaborate surprise party with several hidden stages.

ruptured.definition: Broken open or burst, especially suddenly.
examples: Once the onion's cells are ruptured, the chemical reaction begins.
A ruptured pipe flooded the basement overnight.

volatile.definition: Evaporating easily or changing rapidly, especially describing a chemical or gas.
examples: The onion releases a volatile gas that irritates the eyes.
Prices in the volatile market shifted several times in a single day.

irritation.definition: A feeling of discomfort or slight pain, often caused by an irritant.
examples: The eyes respond to the gas's irritation by producing tears.
The wool sweater caused a mild skin irritation.
Tears help flush the offending compound out of the eyes.
She apologized for the offending remark right away.

plunges.definition: Falls or drops suddenly and steeply.
examples: A stone thrown at the wrong angle simply plunges into the water.
Temperatures often plunge after sunset in the desert.

gyroscope.definition: A spinning device that resists changes to its orientation, helping maintain stability.
examples: A spinning stone behaves a little like a gyroscope in flight.
The ship's navigation system relies on a gyroscope to stay steady.

erratically.definition: In a way that is irregular or unpredictable.
examples: A poorly thrown stone tumbles erratically instead of skipping smoothly.
The old engine ran erratically until it was repaired.

projectiles.definition: Objects thrown or launched through the air, often with force.
examples: Engineers studied skipping stones to design certain naval projectiles.
The catapult launched heavy projectiles over the castle wall.

pastime.definition: An activity done regularly for enjoyment in one's free time.
examples: Skipping stones is a simple pastime with surprisingly complex physics.
Gardening became her favorite pastime after she retired.

flinch.definition: A small, involuntary movement made in reaction to surprise, fear, or discomfort.
examples: Most people flinch the first time they hear their own recorded voice.
He didn't even flinch when the balloon popped beside him.

pathways.definition: Routes or channels through which something travels or is transmitted.
examples: Sound reaches your ears through two separate pathways when you speak.
Nerve pathways carry signals from the skin to the brain.

bypasses.definition: Goes around or avoids something, taking an alternate route.
examples: Bone conduction bypasses the air entirely on its way to the inner ear.
The new highway bypasses the town center completely.

frequencies.definition: Rates at which a sound wave vibrates, determining its pitch.
examples: Bone conduction emphasizes lower frequencies in your self-perceived voice.
The radio can be tuned to different frequencies.

exposure.definition: The condition of being subjected to or experiencing something, often repeatedly.
examples: With repeated exposure, people grow more comfortable with their recorded voice.
Long exposure to loud noise can damage hearing over time.

lagoon.definition: A shallow body of water separated from the sea by a sandbar or reef.
examples: Venice was built on a series of small islands within a lagoon.
Flamingos gathered at the edge of the shallow lagoon.

piles.definition: Long, heavy posts driven into the ground to support a structure's foundation.
examples: Venice's buildings rest on wooden piles driven deep into the mud.
Workers drove steel piles into the riverbed to support the new bridge.

submerged.definition: Completely covered by or placed under water.
examples: The wooden piles have remained submerged for centuries without rotting.
The old shipwreck lay submerged just off the coast.

compressed.definition: Pressed or squeezed into a smaller space or denser form.
examples: The piles were driven until they reached a layer of compressed clay.
The files were compressed to save storage space.

improbable.definition: Not likely to happen or be true; unlikely.
examples: Venice remains an improbable city that appears to float on water.
It seemed improbable that the small team would win the championship.

boulder.definition: A large rock, often rounded, sitting on or embedded in the ground.
examples: The road curves gently around a single moss-covered boulder.
Hikers rested in the shade of a huge boulder.

deliberate.definition: Done intentionally and with careful thought, rather than by accident.
examples: The road takes a deliberate detour rather than cutting straight through.
She spoke in a slow, deliberate voice to make sure she was understood.

elusive.definition: Difficult to find, catch, or fully understand.
examples: The huldufólk are described as elusive beings who usually stay out of sight.
A cure for the disease has remained elusive despite years of research.

folklore.definition: Traditional beliefs, stories, and customs passed down within a community.
examples: Icelandic folklore holds that hidden people live inside certain rocks.
The village's folklore includes tales of a ghost in the old mill.

curiosity.definition: Something that arouses interest because it is unusual or intriguing.
examples: The oddly bent road has become a minor tourist curiosity.
The antique shop was full of strange curiosities from around the world.

intrigued.definition: Very interested or curious about something.
examples: Intrigued by the pattern, Zeigarnik designed experiments to test it further.
She was intrigued by the strange noise coming from the attic.
Participants were deliberately interrupted partway through some of the tasks.
He deliberately left his phone at home to avoid distractions.

interrupted.definition: Stopped temporarily or broken into before completion.
examples: The interrupted tasks were remembered far more often than the finished ones.
The meeting was interrupted by a sudden fire alarm.

cliffhanger.definition: A dramatic, suspenseful ending that leaves an outcome unresolved.
examples: The Zeigarnik effect helps explain why a television cliffhanger is so hard to ignore.
The novel ended on a cliffhanger that left readers desperate for the sequel.

resolved.definition: Settled, decided, or brought to a conclusion.
examples: The mental tension stays active until the unfinished task is resolved.
The dispute was finally resolved after months of negotiation.

upscale.definition: Relating to or intended for wealthier or higher-status customers; higher in quality or price.
examples: Researchers set up their jam display inside an upscale grocery store.
The upscale restaurant required reservations weeks in advance.

array.definition: A wide and impressive range or display of things.
examples: Shoppers were drawn in by the sheer array of jam flavors.
The market offered an array of fresh vegetables from local farms.

paralyze.definition: To make someone unable to move, act, or decide.
examples: Too many options can paralyze decision-making rather than help it.
Fear of failure sometimes paralyzes people before they even begin.

replicated.definition: Repeated or reproduced closely, especially in an experiment or study.
examples: Researchers have replicated the jam-choice effect across many other decisions.
The experiment was replicated in three different countries with similar results.

counterintuitively.definition: In a way that is opposite to what one would naturally expect.
examples: Counterintuitively, offering fewer choices sometimes leads to more sales.
Counterintuitively, the slower route turned out to save them time overall.

fleet.definition: A group of vehicles, ships, or in this case satellites, operating together under one system.
examples: A fleet of satellites broadcasts the precise time signals GPS depends on.
The company operates a fleet of delivery trucks across the city.

precision.definition: The quality of being exact and accurate.
examples: GPS location depends on extreme precision in timing signals.
The surgeon worked with remarkable precision during the operation.

relativistic.definition: Relating to Einstein's theory of relativity and its effects on time, space, or motion.
examples: Relativistic effects cause satellite clocks to run at a different rate than clocks on Earth.
At very high speeds, relativistic effects become impossible to ignore.

negligible.definition: So small or unimportant that it is not worth considering.
examples: A few microseconds a day sounds negligible, but it isn't for GPS.
The difference in price between the two options was negligible.

compound.definition: To increase gradually in size or effect through repeated addition.
examples: Left uncorrected, the tiny timing error would compound into a large navigation drift.
Small daily savings compound into a significant amount over the years.

consumes.definition: Uses up a resource, such as energy or time.
examples: Every email consumes a small amount of electricity as it travels through the network.
The old refrigerator consumes far more power than a newer model.

attachments.definition: Files sent along with an email or message.
examples: Large attachments increase the energy needed to send and store an email.
She forgot to include the attachments before sending the message.

emissions.definition: Substances, especially gases, released into the environment.
examples: Spam accounts for a surprisingly large share of total email-related emissions.
The city set new limits on vehicle emissions.

nudge.definition: A gentle push or prompt toward a particular action or outcome.
examples: Small daily habits nudge a mostly invisible cost a little closer to zero.
A friendly reminder was enough to nudge him into finishing the report.

footprint.definition: The measurable impact something has, especially on the environment.
examples: Researchers study the carbon footprint of everyday digital habits like email.
Reducing water use is one way to shrink your household's footprint.

invariably.definition: In every case or on every occasion; always.
examples: The train is invariably late on rainy mornings.
He invariably orders the same dish at the restaurant.

distinguish.definition: To recognize or point out a difference between people or things.
examples: It is hard to distinguish the twins from one another.
The software can distinguish between human speech and background noise.

hues.definition: Colors or shades of a color.
examples: The sunset painted the sky in warm hues of orange and pink.
The artist used different hues of blue to create depth.

wavelengths.definition: The distances between successive crests of a wave, especially in light or sound.
examples: Different wavelengths of light produce different colors.
The radio tower transmits signals on multiple wavelengths.

stimulate.definition: To raise levels of physiological or nervous activity in the body.
examples: Bright lights stimulate the brain and make it harder to sleep.
The new project helped stimulate his interest in science.

subterranean.definition: Existing, occurring, or done under the earth's surface.
examples: The river flowed through a subterranean cavern.
Ants build complex subterranean chambers.

nutrients.definition: Substances that provide nourishment essential for growth and the maintenance of life.
examples: Healthy soil is rich in nutrients needed for crops.
Plants absorb water and nutrients through their roots.

seedlings.definition: Young plants, especially ones raised from seed rather than from a cutting.
examples: The gardener planted the tomato seedlings in small pots.
Forest fires can destroy young seedlings before they grow strong.

mutual.definition: Held in common by two or more parties, or done by each toward the other.
examples: The agreement was based on mutual trust.
Their friendship was built on a mutual love of music.

superorganism.definition: An organized society or collection of organisms that functions as a single organic whole.
examples: A beehive is a classic example of a superorganism.
Some biologists view the entire forest as a giant superorganism.

inherited.definition: Received from predecessors or past generations.
examples: The young nation inherited a complex legal system.
She inherited a collection of antique books from her grandfather.

imprint.definition: To produce a mark or pattern on a surface by pressure.
examples: The heavy boots would imprint deep tracks in the mud.
A metal stamp is used to imprint the company logo on paper.

untangle.definition: To free from tangles or complications.
examples: It took him several minutes to untangle the fishing line.
She tried to untangle the confusing sequence of events.

ergonomic.definition: Designed for efficiency and comfort in the working environment.
examples: The office purchased ergonomic chairs to reduce back strain.
An ergonomic keyboard can help prevent wrist pain.
We had to consider other alternatives when the flight was cancelled.
Scientists are searching for clean energy alternatives to coal.

inherent.definition: Existing in something as a permanent, essential, or characteristic attribute.
examples: There are inherent risks in any major scientific expedition.
The love of play seems inherent in young mammals.
The jagged rocks along the coast were dangerous for ships.
He cut his hand on the jagged edge of the broken glass.

consensus.definition: A general agreement among a group of people.
examples: The committee reached a consensus after hours of debate.
There is no scientific consensus on the cause of the phenomenon.

crossmodal.definition: Involving the interaction of two or more sensory modalities, such as sight and hearing.
examples: Synesthesia is a rare form of cross-modal perception.
The experiment demonstrated a cross-modal link between sound and shape.

arbitrary.definition: Based on random choice or personal whim, rather than any reason or system.
examples: The selection of the winner seemed entirely arbitrary.
Rules shouldn't be arbitrary; they need a clear purpose.

transactions.definition: Instances of buying or selling something, or business deals.
examples: Online banking allows fast and secure financial transactions.
The merchant recorded all daily transactions in a ledger.

logistically.definition: Relating to the organization and planning of a complex operation.
examples: Moving the entire museum collection was logistically challenging.
The project was logistically difficult but completed on time.

commerce.definition: The activity of buying and selling, especially on a large scale.
examples: The new port was built to facilitate international commerce.
Internet commerce has grown rapidly over the last decade.

acknowledged.definition: Accepted or admitted the truth or existence of something.
examples: The scientist acknowledged that further research was necessary.
He acknowledged the applause from the crowd with a bow.

testified.definition: Gave evidence as a witness, or served as proof or evidence of something.
examples: The witness testified under oath during the trial.
Several survivors testified to the severity of the storm.

microscopic.definition: So small as to be visible only with a microscope.
examples: The water was full of microscopic algae.
A microscopic crack in the pipe caused the leak.

rigid.definition: Stiff and unyielding; not able to be bent or forced out of shape.
examples: The tent was supported by a rigid steel frame.
Ice forms a rigid structure when water molecules freeze.

wobble.definition: Move unsteadily from side to side.
examples: The old table began to wobble when he leaned on it.
The spinning top started to wobble as it slowed down.
The room was disordered after the children played there.
A disordered layer of molecules coats the surface of ice.
The painting has high historical value, regardless of its intrinsic worth.
Curiosity is an intrinsic part of human nature.

bewildered.definition: Confused or perplexed by something complex or unexpected.
examples: The tourists were bewildered by the city's maze of streets.
She looked bewildered when asked to solve the complex puzzle.

soared.definition: Increased rapidly above the usual level.
examples: Temperatures soared to record heights during the heatwave.
Stock prices soared after the company announced its new product.

haze.definition: A slight obscurity in the air, caused by dust, smoke, or light vapor.
examples: A thick haze hung over the valley on the hot afternoon.
The volcanic eruption created a high-altitude haze.

famine.definition: Extreme scarcity of food, leading to widespread hunger.
examples: The failure of the potato crop caused a severe famine.
International aid was sent to prevent a widespread famine.

gloomy.definition: Dark or poorly lit, especially so as to appear depressing or frightening.
examples: The gloomy forest was quiet and dark.
A gloomy rainy day made them want to stay inside.

endure.definition: Suffer patiently or remain in existence through difficult conditions.
examples: The old oak tree has managed to endure many harsh winters.
Explorers must endure extreme cold to reach the South Pole.
The weather began to deteriorate as the storm approached.
Without maintenance, the old building will slowly deteriorate.

metabolism.definition: The chemical processes that occur within a living organism in order to maintain life.
examples: A warm environment can speed up an animal's metabolism.
Hibernation slows a bear's metabolism to save energy.

fluid.definition: A substance, such as a liquid or gas, that has no fixed shape and yields to external pressure.
examples: The mechanic checked the brake fluid in the car.
Cellular fluid must be protected from freezing.

rehydrates.definition: Absorbs water again after being dehydrated.
examples: The dried fruit rehydrates when soaked in warm water.
A dehydrated athlete rehydrates by drinking fluids.

auditory.definition: Relating to the sense of hearing.
examples: The auditory nerve carries signals from the ear to the brain.
He preferred auditory learning, like listening to podcasts.

integration.definition: The act or process of combining separate parts into a whole.
examples: The integration of new technology made the factory more efficient.
The brain is responsible for the integration of sensory inputs.
The witnesses gave conflicting accounts of the accident.
She had conflicting feelings about accepting the new job.

overriding.definition: Being more important or powerful than other factors; dominating.
examples: Safety is the overriding concern for the engineering team.
The visual input ended up overriding the auditory signal.

cues.definition: Signals or prompts that act as a guide to behavior or interpretation.
examples: Actors rely on visual cues to know when to enter the stage.
The brain uses subtle cues to recognize emotions in a voice.

ravines.definition: Deep, narrow gorges with steep sides, especially ones worn by water.
examples: The hikers carefully crossed the narrow bridge over the ravines.
Rainwater carved deep ravines into the clay hills.

mimic.definition: Imitate or copy the behavior, sound, or appearance of something.
examples: Certain insects mimic leaves to avoid being eaten by birds.
The whistlers use pitch changes to mimic spoken words.

terrain.definition: A stretch of land, especially with regard to its physical features.
examples: The rocky terrain made hiking slow and difficult.
The vehicle was designed to handle rough, mountainous terrain.

arduous.definition: Involving or requiring strenuous effort; difficult and tiring.
examples: Climbing the mountain was an arduous journey that took all day.
They faced the arduous task of clearing the forest path.

compulsory.definition: Required by law or a rule; obligatory.
examples: In many countries, primary education is compulsory for all children.
The school made physical education a compulsory class.

active.definition: Functioning by producing its own signal or energy.
examples: Active noise cancellation generates its own sound waves to block noise.
Volcanoes that are active can erupt at any time.

fundamental.definition: Serving as a system's core or most important basis.
examples: The laws of gravity are fundamental to understanding physics.
Clean water is a fundamental human need.

ambient.definition: Relating to the immediate surroundings or environment.
examples: The microphone measures ambient noise levels in the room.
The restaurant had soft, ambient lighting.

destructive.definition: Resulting in the cancellation of waves when opposing peaks meet.
examples: The storm's destructive winds damaged several roofs.
Destructive interference occurs when two opposing waves cancel out.

trough.definition: The lowest point of a wave.
examples: A wave is defined by its high crest and its low trough.
Water filled the drinking trough for the horses.

contest.definition: Oppose or challenge an argument, theory, or claim.
examples: The losing candidate decided to contest the election results.
Nations often contest borders in resource-rich areas.

habitable.definition: Suitable or good enough to live in.
examples: The old house was barely habitable after years of neglect.
Scientists search for habitable planets in other solar systems.

trapezoidshaped.definition: Having the form of a quadrilateral with only one pair of parallel sides.
examples: The architect designed a trapezoid-shaped window for the roof.
The disputed territory is a trapezoid-shaped piece of land.

fertile.definition: Producing or capable of producing abundant vegetation or crops.
examples: The river valley is known for its fertile soil.
A fertile imagination can help a writer create rich stories.

forfeits.definition: Loses or gives up a right or property as a consequence of a choice or rule.
examples: A team that fails to show up forfeits the match.
Accepting the smaller territory forfeits the claim to the larger one.

genetically.definition: In a way that relates to genes or genetics.
examples: The twins are genetically identical.
The crops were genetically modified to resist pests.

ripening.definition: Becoming mature and ready to eat, especially describing fruit.
examples: The warm sun accelerated the ripening of the apples.
Bananas are harvested green to control their ripening speed.

uniformity.definition: The quality or state of being uniform; consistency or lack of variation.
examples: The factory prioritized uniformity to ensure high product quality.
A lack of genetic uniformity makes the crop vulnerable to disease.

sweeter.definition: Having a taste like sugar; more sweet.
examples: Wild strawberries are often sweeter than cultivated ones.
The older variety of banana was sweeter than the modern one.

sturdier.definition: Stronger and more solid; less easily broken.
examples: The new bridge was built to be sturdier than the old wooden one.
The older banana variety was sturdier and easier to ship.

defies.definition: Openly resists or goes against expectations.
examples: The acrobat's performance defies gravity.
This luxury good defies the standard laws of economics.

utility.definition: The state of being useful, profitable, or beneficial.
examples: The tool has high utility despite its simple design.
Consumers evaluate a product based on its utility and price.

conspicuous.definition: Clearly visible or attracting attention, often designed to show off.
examples: The large sign was placed in a conspicuous position.
The wealthy merchant engaged in conspicuous consumption to show off his success.

consumption.definition: The purchase and use of goods or resources.
examples: Industrial energy consumption has increased this year.
Conspicuous consumption is driven by a desire for social status.

deterrent.definition: A thing that discourages or is intended to discourage someone from doing something.
examples: A high price is usually a deterrent for most shoppers.
A security system serves as a deterrent to thieves.
A sudden bolt of lightning lit up the night sky.
The bolt struck a tall tree in the field.

accumulates.definition: Gathers together or acquires an increasing quantity of something.
examples: Dust accumulates quickly on shelves if they aren't cleaned.
Static electricity accumulates in clouds during a storm.

induces.definition: Brings about or causes something to happen.
examples: A cold environment induces shivering to keep the body warm.
The negative charge in the cloud induces a positive charge on the ground.

stepped.definition: Progressing by short stages or steps.
examples: A stepped leader is a downward path of electrical charge.
The pathway had a stepped design leading down to the garden.

surges.definition: Moves suddenly and powerfully forward or upward.
examples: Water surges through the broken dam.
A massive electrical current surges from the ground to the cloud.
